title: Exercises: Decoherence
description: Hands-on exercises to understand decoherence in quantum systems

exercises.title: Simulate Dephasing Channel
exercises.description: Implement a dephasing channel and observe how coherence decays over time.
exercises.starterCode:
import numpy as np

def dephasing_channel(rho, gamma):
    """
    Apply dephasing to a density matrix.
    gamma: dephasing strength (0 to 1)
    """
    # TODO: Multiply off-diagonal elements by (1-gamma)
    result = rho.copy()
    # Your code here
    return result

# Create |+⟩ state density matrix
plus = np.array([[1], [1]]) / np.sqrt(2)
rho = plus @ plus.conj().T

print('Initial state:')
print(rho)

# Apply increasing dephasing
for gamma in [0.0, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1.0]:
    result = dephasing_channel(rho, gamma)
    print(f'\ngamma = {gamma}:')
    print(result)
exercises.solution:
import numpy as np

def dephasing_channel(rho, gamma):
    result = rho.copy()
    result[0, 1] *= (1 - gamma)
    result[1, 0] *= (1 - gamma)
    return result

plus = np.array([[1], [1]]) / np.sqrt(2)
rho = plus @ plus.conj().T

print('Initial state:')
print(np.round(rho, 3))

for gamma in [0.0, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1.0]:
    result = dephasing_channel(rho, gamma)
    print(f'\ngamma = {gamma}:')
    print(np.round(result, 3))
hints: Off-diagonal elements are rho[0,1] and rho[1,0]
exercises.expectedOutput: Off-diagonal elements decay from 0.5 to 0 as gamma increases

exercises.title: Calculate Algorithm Feasibility
exercises.description: Given coherence time and gate time, calculate how many operations can run before decoherence.
exercises.starterCode:
import numpy as np

def calculate_circuit_feasibility(T2_us, gate_time_ns, circuit_depth, n_qubits):
    """
    Calculate if a circuit can run before decoherence.
    
    T2_us: coherence time in microseconds
    gate_time_ns: gate time in nanoseconds
    circuit_depth: number of gate layers
    n_qubits: number of qubits
    
    Returns: success probability
    """
    # TODO: Convert units
    T2 = # microseconds to seconds
    gate_time = # nanoseconds to seconds
    
    # TODO: Calculate total circuit time
    circuit_time = # Your code
    
    # TODO: Calculate error probability per qubit
    # error = 1 - exp(-circuit_time / T2)
    
    # TODO: Calculate overall success probability
    # All qubits must survive: (1-error)^n_qubits
    
    return success_prob

# Test scenarios
scenarios = [
    (100, 20, 100, 10),   # Easy
    (100, 20, 1000, 50),  # Medium
    (100, 20, 10000, 100), # Hard
]

for T2, gate, depth, qubits in scenarios:
    prob = calculate_circuit_feasibility(T2, gate, depth, qubits)
    print(f'T2={T2}μs, depth={depth}, qubits={qubits}: {prob:.2%}')
exercises.solution:
import numpy as np

def calculate_circuit_feasibility(T2_us, gate_time_ns, circuit_depth, n_qubits):
    T2 = T2_us * 1e-6
    gate_time = gate_time_ns * 1e-9
    
    circuit_time = circuit_depth * gate_time
    error_per_qubit = 1 - np.exp(-circuit_time / T2)
    success_prob = (1 - error_per_qubit) ** n_qubits
    
    return success_prob

scenarios = [
    (100, 20, 100, 10),
    (100, 20, 1000, 50),
    (100, 20, 10000, 100),
]

for T2, gate, depth, qubits in scenarios:
    prob = calculate_circuit_feasibility(T2, gate, depth, qubits)
    print(f'T2={T2}μs, depth={depth}, qubits={qubits}: {prob:.2%}')
exercises.expectedOutput: Decreasing success probability for deeper circuits

exercises.title: Partial Trace Demonstration
exercises.description: Show how tracing out the environment leads to decoherence by implementing partial trace.
exercises.starterCode:
import numpy as np

def partial_trace_env(rho_full):
    """
    Trace out the environment (second qubit) from a 4x4 density matrix.
    Returns the 2x2 reduced density matrix of the system.
    """
    # TODO: Implement partial trace
    # rho_system[i,j] = sum over k of rho_full[2i+k, 2j+k]
    rho_system = np.zeros((2, 2), dtype=complex)
    # Your code here
    return rho_system

# Create entangled state: (|00⟩ + |11⟩)/√2
bell = np.array([1, 0, 0, 1]) / np.sqrt(2)
rho_full = np.outer(bell, bell.conj())

print('Full density matrix (system + environment):')
print(np.round(rho_full, 3))

rho_system = partial_trace_env(rho_full)
print('\nReduced system density matrix:')
print(np.round(rho_system, 3))
print('\nThis is the maximally mixed state - coherence is lost!')
exercises.solution:
import numpy as np

def partial_trace_env(rho_full):
    rho_system = np.zeros((2, 2), dtype=complex)
    for i in range(2):
        for j in range(2):
            for k in range(2):
                rho_system[i, j] += rho_full[2*i + k, 2*j + k]
    return rho_system

bell = np.array([1, 0, 0, 1]) / np.sqrt(2)
rho_full = np.outer(bell, bell.conj())

print('Full density matrix (system + environment):')
print(np.round(rho_full, 3))

rho_system = partial_trace_env(rho_full)
print('\nReduced system density matrix:')
print(np.round(rho_system, 3))
print('\nThis is the maximally mixed state I/2 - coherence is lost!')
hints: The full Hilbert space is H_sys ⊗ H_env
Use index mapping: full index = 2*sys + env

exercises.title: T1 and T2 Decay Simulation
exercises.description: Simulate and visualize T1 (amplitude decay) and T2 (phase decay) processes.
exercises.starterCode:

    Returns time array, P(|1⟩) array, and coherence array.
    """

    # TODO: T1 decay: P(|1⟩) = exp(-t/T1)
    p1 = # Your code
    
    # TODO: T2 decay: coherence = exp(-t/T2)
    coherence = # Your code
    
    return t, p1, coherence

# Typical values
T1 = 100  # μs
T2 = 50   # μs

t, p1, coh = simulate_decay(T1, T2, 300)

print('Time (μs)\tP(|1⟩)\tCoherence')
for i in range(0, len(t), 20):
    print(f'{t[i]:.0f}\t\t{p1[i]:.3f}\t{coh[i]:.3f}')
exercises.solution:
import numpy as np

def simulate_decay(T1_us, T2_us, total_time_us, n_points=100):
    t = np.linspace(0, total_time_us, n_points)
    p1 = np.exp(-t / T1_us)
    coherence = np.exp(-t / T2_us)
    return t, p1, coherence

T1 = 100
T2 = 50

t, p1, coh = simulate_decay(T1, T2, 300)

print('Time (μs)\tP(|1⟩)\tCoherence')
for i in range(0, len(t), 20):
    print(f'{t[i]:.0f}\t\t{p1[i]:.3f}\t{coh[i]:.3f}')
exercises.expectedOutput: P(|1⟩) decays slower than coherence since T1 > T2
